coredata.dc:title: “Silver effect” enhanced fluorescence for sensitive detection of crystal violet utilizing long wavelength emission bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Congcong
Liu, Can
Zhang, Xu
Tong, Wei
Yin, Yu
Liu, Jinquan
Yang, Shengyuan
coredata.dc:description: Crystal violet (CV), widely used as a fungicide in aquaculture, is susceptible to enrichment in fish and thus poses a threat to human health. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a simple, sensitive and cost-effective method to detect CV. To this need, duck egg white-stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters (DEW-AuAgNCs) with long wavelength fluorescence emission were synthesized via microwave method in 1 min. The raw materials used were easily available and environmentally friendly. And based on the Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between DEW-AuAgNCs and CV, a fluorescence quenching method was established for CV detection. The detection limit of this method was 1.7 × 10−8 mol/L, which was lower than most of the reported methods. In addition, prominent performance of DEW-AuAgNCs with long wavelength emission can be applied for the detection of CVs in real organism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold-silver nanoclusters
Long-wavelength emission
Fluorometric
Crystal violet

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of synthetic conditions for DEW-AuAgNCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS images of DEW-AuAgNCs: survey scan spectrum (A), high-resolution spectra of C 1s (C), O 1s (B), N 1s (D), S 2p (E), Au 4f (F) and Ag 3d (G); FT-IR spectra of DEW-AuAgNCs (H); Fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis spectrum of DEW-AuAgNCs (I).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DEW-AuAgNCs, CV, and DEW-AuAgNCs/CV, (B) the zeta potential diagrams of DEW-AuAgNCs, CV and DEW-AuAgNCs/CV, (C) the excitation spectra (curve a), emission spectra (curve b) of DEW-AuAgNCs and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CV(curve c), (D) the fluorescence lifetimes of DEW-AuAgNCs and DEW-AuAgNCs + CV.
Fluorescence spectra (A) and standard curve (B) for CV detection.
The influence of interfering substances on the CV detection.
Schematic representation of synthesis of DEW-AuAgNCs and CV detection.
Detection results of CV in fish (n = 7).

head.ce:title: “Silver effect” enhanced fluorescence for sensitive detection of crystal violet utilizing long wavelength emission bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan 421001, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan 421001, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213002, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213002, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold-silver nanoclusters (DEW-AuAgNCs) with “silver effect” enhanced fluorescence was synthesized in 1 min. And the detection of crystal violet (CV) in biological samples was achieved based on long wavelength fluorescence emission of DEW-AuAgNCs.

Crystal violet (CV), widely used as a fungicide in aquaculture, is susceptible to enrichment in fish and thus poses a threat to human health. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a simple, sensitive and cost-effective method to detect CV. To this need, duck egg white-stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters (DEW-AuAgNCs) with long wavelength fluorescence emission were synthesized via microwave method in 1 min. The raw materials used were easily available and environmentally friendly. And based on the Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between DEW-AuAgNCs and CV, a fluorescence quenching method was established for CV detection. The detection limit of this method was 1.7 × 10−8 mol/L, which was lower than most of the reported methods. In addition, prominent performance of DEW-AuAgNCs with long wavelength emission can be applied for the detection of CVs in real organism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Crystal violet (CV), a triarylmethane dye, is often used as medical disinfectant, bactericide in aquaculture, and dyeing agent in manufacturing industries such as paper and textiles due to its excellent bactericidal and disinfecting effects [1]. Researchers pointed out CV was difficult to be degraded by microorganisms in nature and would continue to accumulate in the food chain [2]. As a basic cationic dye, it was easily bound to the negatively charged cell membrane surface and entered the cell to be concentrated in the cytoplasm [3,4]. CV could irritate the skin and gastrointestinal tract, and could even cause carcinogenesis, teratogenesis, mutagenesis and other hazards to the human. Considering the aforementioned threat to human health, CV has been banned from use in aquaculture industry [5,6]. However, possessing the advantages of satisfactory bactericidal effect and cost-effectiveness, CV is still used illegally in the aquaculture industry. So far, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) for CV detection has high sensitivity and can analyze multiple components [7,8]. Unfortunately, its dependence on precision instruments limits the application to a certain extent [9]. Therefore, establishing a simple and rapid sensor for detecting residual CV in aquatic products is still challenging.

Fluorometry possesses the virtues of rapidity, simple operation, and high sensitivity, is widely used in the detection of CV [10,11]. Dalavi et al. constructed fluorescent nanoparticles of sodium dodecyl sulphate stabilized 2-naphthol (SDS-2-NNPs), which have a maximum fluorescence emission at 410 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 297 nm. Based on the static quenching mechanism between CV and SDS-2-NNPs, a fluorescent method was established for CV detection in environmental water samples [12]. Nevertheless, the fluorescence of short-wavelength emission probes overlap with the background fluorescence of the biological sample itself, and therefore cannot meet the application requirements in biological samples [9].

Long-wavelength emission fluorescent probes can effectively reduce the background fluorescence interference of biological organisms, providing a basis for the actual detection of biological samples [11]. Researchers have successively developed long-wavelength emission probes, for example, nanoclusters [13], carbon dots [14], fluorescent nanoparticle [15,16] and metal–organic framework [17], and some have been successfully applied to CV detection. Han [15] et al., Yi [18] et al. and Li [11] et al. constructed fluorescence methods for CV detection based on Silicon Nanoparticles (SiNPs), YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles and low-temperature carbon dots (LT-CDs), respectively. However, SiNPs, YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles and LT-CDs all required long synthesis time and the use of radionuclide Eu3+ is not environmentally friendly [18]. Therefore, the design of a rapidly synthesized and eco-friendly probe for CV detection is extremely vital.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) exhibits HOMO-LUMO transition, photoluminescence, wavelength-tunable property, which has more attractive advantages for biochemical sensing in biological samples [19–23]. AuNCs have multiple available templates such as sulfhydryl small molecules, peptides, DNA and proteins [24–27]. Natural proteins are favored for AuNCs synthesis owing to their inexpensiveness and environmental friendliness [28,29]. Unfortunately, the amount of sulfhydryl groups required for AuNCs synthesis from natural proteins is much lower than that of purified proteins, and thus natural proteins-protected AuNCs synthesized show lower fluorescence [30]. Researchers found that Ag atoms have similar sizes and chemical properties to Au atoms, the simultaneous addition of gold and silver will cause a synergistic effect during the synthesis process of nanoclusters, making the bimetallic nanoclusters exhibit stronger fluorescence [28]. Based on the aforementioned property, several highly fluorescent protein-protected AuAgNCs have been developed [30–32]. However, their synthesis took too long time, further work is still needed on the rapid synthesis of protein-protected AuAgNCs.

Herein, gold-silver nanoclusters (DEW-AuAgNCs), possessing high fluorescence and long-wavelength fluorescence emission, were synthesized in 1 min with a template of the biomass material duck egg white (DEW) via microwave-assisted method. And the fluorescence of DEW-AuAgNCs could be quenched by CV attributing to Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Scheme 1 ). In view of the changes in the fluorescence of DEW-AuAgNCs, a simple and rapid method for CV detection was established, and successfully applied to the residual CV detection in aquatic products.

Luminous Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), UV2550 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (Oregon, USA), FLS980 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), IRTracer-100 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK), Escalab 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

NaCl, Al2(SO4)3·18H2O, KCl, ZnCl2, CaCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, FA, VB2, VB12, GSH, L-cysteine, glucose, crystal violet and malachite green (MG) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). AgNO3, Lead (Pb) standard solution, and HAc were acquired from Sinopharm Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4·4H2O were purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co.,Ltd. (Beijing, China). Duck eggs were purchased from the local market. All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade. The water was ultrapure water with the resistance ≥18.25 MΩ∙cm.

Natural proteins duck egg white, HAuCl4·4H2O and AgNO3 were used as precursors for the rapid synthesis of DEW-AuAgNCs via a one-step microwave method in one minute. The duck egg whites were separated, magnetically stirred for 10 min, and then sonicated for 20 min to mix sufficiently for use. 1.815 mL of ultrapure water, 1.7 mL duck egg white, 2.4 mL 5.0 × 10−3 mol/L HAuCl4, 0.185 mL 5.0 × 10−3 mol/L AgNO3 were added sequentially, magnetic stirred for 2 min. Then 500 µL of 1.0 mol/L NaOH was added dropwise, stirred for 2 min, and reacted for 1 min in a microwave oven at 225 W to obtain DEW-AuAgNCs.

DEW-AuAgNCs were purified by centrifugation at 4000 r/min for 20 min using a 50 KDa ultrafiltration tube. Then the filtrate was discarded, and the concentrated solution was taken for Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FT-IR) characterizations.

100 µL NH4Ac-HAc buffer (pH 5.0), 50 µL DEW-AuAgNCs and 50 µL CV standard solutions of different concentrations were added in sequence. After reacting at 30 °C for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were scanned. The negative high voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set to 700 V and the slit was 10 nm. Under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity at 682 nm was recorded.

Crucian carp and grass carp were used as test samples to evaluate the practicality of this method. The fish flesh was crushed by a tissue grinder. 5 g of the above-ground fish meat was accurately weighed, 13 mL of acetonitrile was added, sonicated for 30 min, and centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. Another 12 mL of acetonitrile was added to the precipitate, and the above operation was repeated. Then the two extracts were combined, the acetonitrile was evaporated, and 25 mL of ultrapure water was added for CV detection, and a recovery experiment was also performed.

DEW-AuAgNCs were synthesized via a one-step microwave method using duck egg white as a protective agent and reducing agent. The formation process of DEW-AuAgNCs could be divided into three steps. Firstly, in alkaline environments, AuCl4 − and Ag+ can be reduced to gold and silver atoms by the reductive components of duck egg white (tyrosine and cysteine residues). Since the reduction potential of AuCl4 − is lower than that of Ag+, the reduction rate of AuCl4 − is relatively faster than that of Ag+, and thus the reduced Au atoms are deposited to form gold nuclei more rapidly than silver atoms. Then Ag+ forms a metallic bond on the surface of the formed gold nuclei, meanwhile the gold nuclei catalyze the deposition of silver nuclei. Finally, gold nuclei and silver nuclei are co-deposited thereby forming alloy nuclei and further aggregated inside the template to form gold and silver nanoclusters (DEW-AuAgNCs) with strong fluorescence emission [30].

TEM was used to observe the particle size and morphology of DEW-AuAgNCs. As shown in Fig. S1, it could be seen that DEW-AuAgNCs were uniformly dispersed in the aqueous solution and had a nearly spherical shape without a core–shell structure. The inset in Fig. S1A showed the lattice structure of DEW-AuAgNCs, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.19 nm. The particle size distribution diagram of DEW-AuAgNCs shows that its particle size was 1.1 ∼ 2.5 nm, with an average size of approximately 1.83 nm (Fig. S1B).

XPS was employed to further analyze the elements and valence states on the surface of DEW-AuAgNCs. From Fig. 1 A, it could be seen that the surface of DEW-AuAgNCs mainly included six elements, C, N, O, S, Au and Ag, respectively. Fig. 1B showed that the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s could be divided into three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.2 eV, and 289.2 eV, which correspond to CC, C-N/C-O/C-S, and CO/CS/CN, respectively [31]. The characteristic peaks of O 1s at 531.0 eV and 532.1 eV could be attributed to CO and C-O, respectively, and the absorption peak of ambient oxygen is at 535.7 eV (Fig. 1C) [32]. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum showed the characteristic peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.0 eV, corresponding to N-H and C-N-C groups, respectively (Fig. 1D) [33]. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1E) indicated three energy peaks located at 163.0 eV, 168.6 eV, and 170.0 eV, corresponding to the –SH/-S-S-, –SO3, and –SO-S-/-SO2-S- bonds [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of Au 4f was divided into Au 4f7/2 (84.1 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (88.1 eV) (Fig. 1F), which were the characteristic absorption peaks of Au(0) and Au(I), respectively [35], suggesting that most of AuCl4 − has been reduced to Au(0). In addition, the literature reported that the presence of Au(I) on the surface of gold cores has the effect of stabilizing the nanoclusters [36]. The two absorption peaks at 366.8 eV and 375.5 eV observed in the high-resolution spectrum of Ag 3d belonged to Ag(I) and Ag(0), respectively (Fig. 1G) [30]. The above results of the XPS indicated the successful preparation of DEW-AuAgNCs.

The chemical structure and formation mechanism of DEW-AuAgNCs were investigated by FT-IR. Fig. 1H showed the FT-IR spectra of duck egg white and DEW-AuAgNCs, revealing that the absorption peaks of the two were extremely similar. The absorption peak at 3298 cm−1 was related to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [37]. The characteristic peak at 1531 cm−1 attributed to C-N and the vibrational absorption peak at 1649 cm−1 belonging to CO, which proved the presence of amide I and amide II bands, respectively. After synthesizing DEW-AuAgNCs, the position of absorption peaks belonging to the amide bonds did not change significantly, indicating that the synthesized AuAgNCs were encapsulated in duck egg white [38].

To explore the optical properties of DEW-AuAgNCs, the UV–visible absorption spectra were scanned. As shown in Fig. 1I (green line), a small absorption peak at 280 nm was appeared, which mainly originates from disulfide bonds and aromatic residues (tryptophan, tyrosine and cysteine residues) in duck egg white. There were no plasma resonance peaks at 450 nm and 520 nm, indicating that no gold nanoparticles and silver nanoparticles were formed [38].

The excitation and emission spectra of DEW-AuAgNCs were also scanned. As shown by the black and red lines in Fig. 1I, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 334 nm and 682 nm, respectively, suggesting a large Stokes shift (348 nm) and long-wavelength fluorescence emission. And it presented a dark yellow color in natural light and a strong red fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1I inset). In addition, to demonstrate that it was the synergistic effect of gold and silver that greatly increased the fluorescence intensity of DEW-AuAgNCs, DEW-AuNCs and DEW-AgNCs were synthesized under the same conditions. And the emission spectra were scanned at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. S2). The results showed that the fluorescence of DEW-AuNCs synthesized by duck egg white and HAuCl4 was weak (red line of Fig. S2), DEW-AgNCs synthesized by duck egg white and AgNO3 have no fluorescence (blue line of Fig. S2), and the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized DEW-AuAgNCs was greatly increased when only HAuCl4 and AgNO3 were present simultaneously (black line of Fig. S2). This demonstrated the enhancement of product fluorescence by the synergistic effect of HAuCl4 and AgNO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthetic conditions for DEW-AuAgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the thiol groups of tyrosine residues and cysteine residues in duck egg white acted as reducing and stabilizing agents, and the amounts affected the size and the fluorescence intensity of DEW-AuAgNCs. Simultaneously, HAuCl4 and AgNO3 were critical for the formation of gold and silver nuclei. However, too little AgNO3 was not enough to enhance the fluorescence of DEW-AuAgNCs to the maximum extent, while too much AgNO3 rather decreased the fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the exposure of tyrosine residues and cysteine residues in duck egg white required the assistance of NaOH. But non-fluorescent nanoparticles with large sizes were easily formed when the NaOH concentration was too large. Besides, microwaves provided the energy for the reaction and greatly reduce the reaction time. Unfortunately, too long time also tended to form non-fluorescent nanoparticles. Therefore, the synthetic conditions, the concentration of protein, the molar ratio of HAuCl4 and AgNO3, the microwave time and the amount of NaOH, were explored. As shown in Figs. S3–S6, the fluorescence intensity was maximized when the volume of duck egg white was 1.7 mL, the molar ratio of HAuCl4 and AgNO3 was 13:1, the concentration of NaOH was 7.6 × 10−2 mol/L, and the microwave time was 60 s.

Photoinduced electron transfer (PET), electrostatic interactions, FRET and internal filtering effect (IFE) are common mechanisms of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, it was systematically validated by a series of characterizations such as UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime.

Fig. 2 A showed the UV–Vis absorption spectra of DEW-AuAgNCs, CV, and DEW-AuAgNCs/CV. Comparing DEW-AuAgNCs, the UV–Vis absorption peak position of DEW-AuAgNCs/CV did not appear new absorption peaks, so the reaction between DEW-AuAgNCs and CV did not produce a new complex and PET was not the factor leading to fluorescence quenching of DEW-AuAgNCs [39].

As shown in Fig. 2B were the zeta potential diagrams of DEW-AuAgNCs, CV and DEW-AuAgNCs/CV. The zeta potentials were −18.43 eV, −6.29 eV and −21.5 eV, respectively, all of which were negatively charged, so no electrostatic interactions occurred [40].

The occurrence of FRET requires the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the acceptor, and results in weakened fluorescence of the donor and decay of the fluorescence lifetime [41]. Whereas the occurrence of IFE requires that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of quencher overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the donor, and does not result in the decay of the fluorescence lifetime [42]. Scanning of the excitation and emission spectra (curve a and b in Fig. 2C) and UV–Vis absorption spectrum (curve c in Fig. 2C) revealed a partial overlap between the emission spectrum of DEW-AuAgNCs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CV, suggesting that FRET or IFE may have occurred. Thus the fluorescence lifetimes of DEW-AuAgNCs and DEW-AuAgNCs, CV were further examined. The fluorescence lifetime of DEW-AuAgNCs was 405 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of DEW-AuAgNCs/CV decayed to 282 ns, which manifested a significant decay of the fluorescence lifetime after the addition of CV (Fig. 2D). In summary, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was FRET between DEW-AuAgNCs and CV.

The buffer type, pH, reaction temperature and reaction time were optimized to obtain the best assay performance. The results were presented in Figs. S7–S10, in NH4Ac-HAc buffer (pH 5), maximum ΔF/F 0 (F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of CV, respectively) were obtained when DEW-AuAgNCs and CV were reacted at 30 °C for 5 min.

To assess the sensitivity of the method, standard curves were plotted under optimal experimental conditions. Under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence value at 682 nm of the system decreased with increasing CV concentration (Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF/F 0) was linearly correlated with the concentration of CV in the range of 5.0 × 10−8 ∼ 1.4 × 10−6 mol/L. The standard curve equation was y = 0.39x + 0.07, and the correlation coefficient was R2 = 0.9992. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.7 × 10−8 mol/L according to the formula LOD = 3Sb/k suggested by IUPAC. The CV standard solutions at three concentrations (5.0 × 10−8, 6.0 × 10−7 and 1.2 × 10−6 mol/L) were determined simultaneously for seven times, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 4.99 %, 0.10 % and 2.29 %, respectively, which indicated that the method have excellent precision. Comparison of several reported CV detection methods, as shown in Table S1, indicated that the established method has high sensitivity.

To evaluate the specificity of the prepared assay for CV, the response of the fluorescent probe to common interferences in fish samples such as ions, glucose, vitamins, small molecules, and malachite green (MG) was investigated. The relative errors were controlled within ±5 %. As shown in Fig. 4 , comparing the CVs of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L, it was found that 1000-fold K+, Al3+, Na+, NO3 −, Cl−, GSH and L-cys, 500-fold Zn2+, Ca2+, SO4 2−, Glu, FA and VB2, 200-fold Fe3+ and Pb2+, 100-fold VB12, 20-fold Cu2+, 5-fold MG, and 1-fold Cr3+ were all unresponsive to the fluorescent probe. Therefore, the established method has good selectivity for CV.

To further validate the practicality of the method, the levels of CV in grass carp and crucian carp were examined and spiked recovery tests were also performed. As shown in Table 1 , CV was not detected in grass carp and crucian carp. And the spiked recoveries were 98.46 % ∼ 104.90 %. Furthermore, we detected the spiked samples using the standard method (LC-MS/MS, high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry) to verify the accuracy of the method for detecting CV (Table S2). Therefore, the fluorescent probe constructed in this study was accurate and reliable for the detection of CV in fish samples.

In conclusion, a cost-effectiveness natural protein was selected as template for rapidly synthesis of DEW-AuAgNCs in 1 min. The synergistic effect of Au and Ag greatly enhanced the fluorescence of DEW-AuAgNCs. In addition, possessing long wavelength fluorescence emission, the proposed fluorescent probe could effectively avoid the background fluorescence of biological samples. Based on the FRET between DEW-AuAgNCs and CV, a fluorescence quenching method was established to detect CV. The method has a wide linear range, low detection limit, and satisfactory selectivity and has been successfully applied in the detection of residual CV in biological samples.

Congcong Hu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Can Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xu Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Wei Tong: Formal analysis. Yu Yin: Formal analysis. Jinquan Liu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Shengyuan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.82073604, 82304195)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111304.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
